10. Sufficient Statistics

Data comes from very large alphabet (in discrete case) or has very high dimension (in continuous-valued case). When is extracting features lossless (sufficient statistic) and is there a notion of “compactness”? What about Bayesian vs NonBayesian?

10.1. NonBayesian Setting

In the nonBayesian setting, a model family is written as

(10.1)

Define a statistic to be a function of the data. The random variable induces distributions and :

(10.2)

(10.3)

Definition 10.1 (Sufficient Statistic, NonBayesian Form). A statistic for a model family (Equation 10.1) is sufficient if does not depend on , i.e.

(10.4)

The definition expresses that the given , the remaining uncertainty about is not a function of . We cannot infer additional information about by getting access to the value of from which this value of was computed. The reason is because by Equation 10.2 we get the form

(10.5)

I.e. for fixed , the distributions of and are proportional when changing . And with a given observation, the relative variations in the likelihood with are what we have available to exploit in making inferences about the true value of . For example the we get the same likelihood ratio and MLEs if we use .

Remark. An alternative definition would be functional: for any procedure based on , can get the same performance with .

10.4. Minimal Sufficient Statistics

Minimal dimension is not a good notion of minimality without some weird notion of smoothness11.

Definition 10.4 (Minimal Sufficiency). A sufficient statistic is minimal with respect to a model family if for any other sufficient statistic there exists a function such that

i.e. a sufficient statistic is minimal if any other sufficient statistic can be processed into it.

Minimality is nonunique up to invertible functions. E.g. if the tuple is minimal so is .

For example, when comes from a finite alphabet, the vector of likelihood ratios is a minimal sufficient statistic (proved in ps4 #1).

is a minimal sufficient statistic for a canonical exponential family, but not in general? See Section 10.6

Denote the likelihood .

[TODO] prove this guy

The intuition is: two observations and contain the same information about the parameter if their likelihood functions differ only by a multiplicative constant independent of .

The minimal statistic can be thought of the maximally coarse partition among sufficient statistic. Partition view quotients about invertible transformations.

Algorithm 10.1 to find minimal sufficient statistic for finite : Organize all with the same normalized likelihood function in to the same partition. Basically like a union find.

10.6. [TODO] Complete Sufficient Statistic

Definition 10.5 (Complete Sufficient Statistic). A sufficient statistic for a model is complete if for any function satisfying

(10.14)

must satisfy almost surely.

Theorem 10.4. A sufficient statistic is minimal if it is complete.

For example, is a complete sufficient statistic for the exponential family based on some Laplace transform theory.
